FEATURED ENTRIES
Previous
Next
DU. CHAIR – DOWN UP GENIUS

The Du Chair is an innovative, mechanical lift-assist armchair designed to facilitate safe and independent sit-to-stand and stand-to-sit movements without the need for electrical power. It enhances muscle memory, reduces trip hazards and is ideal for older people or anyone needing assistance sitting or standing, promoting comfort and safety.

VIEW MORE

INTUITIVE DAVINCI 5 SURGICAL SYSTEM

Da Vinci 5 is the most advanced minimally invasive surgical platform from Intuitive. It was designed to transform surgery to enable better outcomes, more efficiency, and actionable insights to help hospitals lower care costs.

VIEW MORE

VOLDRIVE

VolDrive brings sustainability and hassle-free operation to internet-of-things (IoT) sensors for industrial applications. This innovative device converts motion into electricity, endlessly powering sensors that provide protection and condition monitoring of assets like wind turbines. VolDrive replaces the need for batteries in IoT sensors making them reliable and environmentally sustainable.

VIEW MORE

HURLSTONE MEMORIAL RESERVE COMMUNITY CENTRE

The Hurlstone Memorial Reserve Community Centre provides a hub for community gatherings, a public amenities block, as well as rejuvenation of the park it is set within. The design strives to establish multiple zones for active and passive recreation, through its form and siting.

VIEW MORE

GLENTHORNE NATIONAL PARK – ITYAMAIITPINNA YARTA NATURE PLAYGROUND

Glenthorne National Park-Ityamaiitpinna Yarta Nature Playspace is one of the most extensive nature play spaces in South Australia. It is designed as an all-inclusive, place for children (and adults) of all ages, cultures, degrees of mobility, gender, and neurodivergence to enjoy imaginative and creative play in a natural setting.

VIEW MORE

HIDDEN: SEVEN CHILDREN SAVED

‘Hidden: Seven Children Saved’ at Melbourne Holocaust Museum is a poignant exploration of the Holocaust for 11-14-year-olds. It features stories of hiding to survive and starting anew in Australia. Through youthful, first-person storytelling and hands-on exploration, students learn about prejudice, loss, and kindness, gaining universal lessons in empathy and courage.

VIEW MORE

COMVITA’S PAENGAROA WORKPLACE

Comvita was founded in the 1970’s where the brand’s head office and production facility, once a local pub and shop, was established in the small settlement of Paengaroa. Staff have worked remotely for the past 3 years throughout the pandemic which caused significant cultural challenges.

VIEW MORE

HOW SENDING MAIL ENCOURAGED AUSTRALIANS TO LEARN ABOUT THEIR LOCAL FIRST NATIONS HISTORY

To encourage the ongoing use of Traditional Place names on Australia Post mailing labels, a video series was developed and promoted. It was an emotive and respectful way to connect people to place and to inspire Australians to learn more about their local First Nations history.

VIEW MORE

SUNGAI DESIGN

Swear Words’ brand strategy, identity design and brand applications for ‘Sungai Design’ – a contemporary Balinese furniture design business creating infinitely recyclable products from plastic waste collected from the rivers and waterways throughout Bali and mainland Indonesia.

VIEW MORE

CRYOCARE: REIMAGINING ANTIMICROBIAL DELIVERY IN THE COMMUNITY

CryoCare adopts a patient-centred approach for Hospital-in-the-Home services, enabling community-based antibiotic infusions. The CryoCare flask maintains drug stability throughout 24-hour infusions via embedded phase change material and a vacuum-insulated flask. Designed in response to patient feedback, CryoCare offers a comfortable and flexible solution that integrates into patients’ lives.

VIEW MORE

POLESTAR 3

Polestar 3 is the SUV for the electric age. It challenges traditional notions of what an SUV should be by combining sculpted clean surfacing, minimalistic detailing, and optimised aerodynamics to create a new, technical design language that symbolises a new era in mobility.

VIEW MORE

MONOLITH ALUMINUM

Born of the desire to bring the immersive sound of the Monolith to an ultra compact format and to offer our cutting-edge technology to a wider audience. Crafted with precision by our dedicated team at Nocs, the Monolith Aluminum embodies a blend of minimalist design, audio excellence, and environmental responsibility.

VIEW MORE

SAMSUNG LESS MICROFIBER™ FILTER

The Less Microfiber™ Filter is an external washing machine filter, which can be used with select Samsung front load washing and combo washers & dryers[1]. It helps capture up to 98% of microfibers released during a standard wash cycle[2].

VIEW MORE

FLITE X MARC NEWSON RANGE

Flite x Marc Newson was born from a desire to push the industry forward through an exploration of the limits of eFoil (electric hydrofoil) design, production techniques and materials. It is the culmination of a singular vision to create the world’s best eFoil range through this utterly uncompromising approach.

VIEW MORE

TLK 25 SMART WIFI AND LTE RADIO

TLK 25 provides two-way voice communication, situational awareness, and safety for workers in hospitality, retail, healthcare, and education environments. This solution redefines radio communication as a wearable, intuitive voice-assisted experience delivered over WiFi or LTE.

VIEW MORE

PROLOGUE BY NIDA

Prologue by NIDA is a genre-bending professional development business, designed for a new generation of learners. Applying valuable and surprising insights from the dramatic arts to the workplace, this multi-platform course spans an illustrated book, card games, and accompanying app loaded with short films, audio guides and engaging activities.

VIEW MORE

费用.text:
TIMELINE + FEES

If you have an innovative design project you believe is worthy of recognition, then we want to hear from you. 

Please review the Timeline, Eligibility Criteria and Fees before submitting your entry to the Australian Good Design Awards.

ELIGIBILITY

Eligibility Criteria

The Australian Good Design Awards is an international awards program that accepts entries from all countries and regions. 
Entry must be completed or available on the market prior to the Australian Good Design Awards Ceremony. Projects still under development or construction are not eligible to enter.
Product entries must be complete products, not parts thereof. Individual products sold within a range (not for individual sale) are not eligible to enter – the full range must be submitted for evaluation. A component of a product is not eligible to enter unless it is sold separately. 
Medical and/or therapeutic goods available for sale in Australia must have appropriate Therapeutic Goods Administration (TGA) approval to be eligible for entry. Entries must comply with any applicable standards and/or safety certification (only if required).

Next Gen Category Eligibility Criteria

Entrants must be 30 or under at the time of the Entry Deadline.
Entrants must be Australian or New Zealand citizens or studying at a recognised Australian or New Zealand University, TAFE or secondary college. 
Entries are not required to be commercially available. Concepts and University projects are eligible for entry.
PREPARATION

If you would like to prepare your responses prior to submitting your entry online, we recommend logging in to the Entry Portal and starting an entry. Once you have selected your Category you will be able to download a Blank Entry PDF to help you draft your application.

Please note all entries must be submitted online via the Entry Portal.

AWARDS TIMELINE

Entries Open
03 March 2026

Early Bird Deadline
Fri 20 March 2026

Extended Deadline
Fri 08 May 2026

Judging
May to July 2026

Results Notification
August 2026

Awards Ceremony
Fri 09 October 2026
Grand Ballroom, ICC Sydney

REGISTRATION FEES

Entry to the Australian Good Design Awards incurs a standard Registration Fee, as below. 

DISCIPLINE	REGISTRATION FEE
Next Gen (Student)	Free to Enter
Social Impact / Concept Design	$245 AUD per entry
All Other Disciplines	$495 AUD per entry

Early Bird Discount of 10% automatically applied to all entries submitted before the Early Bird Deadline.  

All fees are excl. GST and may be subject to an additional credit card surcharge.

Winning entries will be automatically included in the Winner’s Package at an additional cost.

WINNER'S PACKAGE FEES

VIEW WINNER'S PACKAGE BENEFITS

The Good Design Winner’s Package provides winners with the opportunity not only to celebrate and promote their success, but to gain recognition and exposure to a global audience. It offers an array of benefits that are designed to help you maximise the impact of your award-winning project.

The Winner’s Package incurs an additional fee, based on the applicant’s company size. For applicants where the Winner’s Package fee may be a barrier to entry (such as, but not exclusive to NFPs, NGOs and start-ups), Good Design Australia offers financial assistance on a case-by-case basis.

COMPANY SIZE	WINNER’S PACKAGE FEE
Start-up (1 Employee)	$750 AUD per winning entry
Small (2-4 Employees)	$1,500 AUD per winning entry
Medium (5-19 Employees)	$2,500 AUD per winning entry
Large (20-99 Employees)	$3,500 AUD per winning entry
Enterprise (100+ Employees)	$4,500 AUD per winning entry

*All fees are excl. GST and may be subject to an additional credit card surcharge.

Winner’s Package Inclusions:

Unrestricted use of Australian Good Design Award registered trademark
Lifetime Project Profile on the Good Design Winner Index
Media Release with personalised Jury Comments
Engraved Australian Good Design Award Trophy
Two Australian Good Design Award Certificates
Feature in Australian Good Design Awards Yearbook
Ongoing promotion through Good Design Australia’s Social Channels
Free Entry into Singapore Good Design Awards – SG Mark
(Please note, this offer relates only to the submission fee, should you be selected as a Winner there are additional costs associated with their Award-Winner Package).
Option to Licence the Korean Good Design Award Trademark
(Please note, this is optional and provided on request. There is an additional cost of $250 USD payable to the KIDP to licence this Trademark).

类别.text:
CATEGORIES & CRITERIA

The Australian Good Design Awards is one of the most diverse international design award programs in the world, comprising 13 overarching Design Disciplines and covering more than 30 Design Categories.

All entries are carefully assessed by an independent expert Jury panel and evaluated against three overarching criteria – each specific to its Design Discipline:

Criteria 1: Good Design
Criteria 2: Design Innovation
Criteria 3: Design Impact 

The Awards are renowned for their rigorous and thorough evaluation process. The Awards uphold an in-depth review of each entry, ensuring each project receives the time and evaluation it deserves. Judging is carried out across three main stages:

Round 1: Online Independent Scoring
Round 2: Group Evaluation
Round 3: Multi-Disciplinary Final Assessment 

BUILT ENVIRONMENT
COMMUNICATION DESIGN
CONCEPT DESIGN
DESIGN RESEARCH
DESIGN STRATEGY
DIGITAL DESIGN
ENGINEERING DESIGN
FASHION AND TEXTILES
NEXT GEN (UNDER 30S)
POLICY DESIGN
PRODUCT DESIGN
SERVICE DESIGN
SOCIAL IMPACT
+ SPECIAL ACCOLADES

BUILT ENVIRONMENT

The Built Environment discipline celebrates excellence in design across commercial, residential, interior, exterior, landscape, public space, exhibition, urban projects and precincts and cities. With a focus on good design principles, it recognises work that delivers a positive impact on people, place, and planet – from small-scale interiors to large precincts and city planning.

SUB-CATEGORIES INCLUDE:

1. ARCHITECTURAL DESIGN – Includes Commercial Architecture, Public Architecture and Residential Architecture

2. INSTALLATION DESIGN – Includes Exhibition Design, Temporary Installation Design and Experiential Design

3. INTERIOR DESIGN – Includes Commercial Interior, Public Interior and Residential Interior

4. PLACE DESIGN – Includes Urban Design, Landscape Architecture, Public Spaces, Precinct Design and City Planning

EVALUATION CRITERIA

GOOD DESIGN

Describe how the project meets the criteria for good design in a Built Environment sense including:

Form – is the design visually resolved and does it respond to its environment from a visual, contextual and cultural perspective?

Function – does the design perform the function it was designed for, is it easy to navigate and use and does it advance the overall user experience?

Safety – has the project been designed with appropriate safety considerations and does it comply with all applicable safety standards and building regulations?

Quality – has the project been well constructed with appropriate materials and construction techniques and does the quality of the project correspond with the designed price point and/or investment?

Commerciality – does the project represent good value for money at the intended price point and/or investment, is the design likely to increase the brand value and/or community standing of the client organisation and is it likely to result in a positive economic and/or social return on the investment made on design?

DESIGN INNOVATION

Describe how the project sets a new benchmark for innovation in a Built Environment practice.

The Jury wants to understand if the project incorporates any leading-edge Built Environment innovations, if it advances the profession and pushes the boundaries of new and innovative thinking in Built Environment practice.

Is the project truly unique, imaginative and innovative and does it offer a new and ground-breaking value proposition with potential world-first features? Does the project use new systems, building materials, construction techniques and processes and/or technology in a clever, imaginative and original manner?

DESIGN IMPACT

Describe the overall impact of the project and what long-lasting and positive impact will be achieved for the client, the end-users and the environment.

More specifically, the Jury want to understand what measurable outcomes have been achieved and if the design of the space, place or environment has the potential to improve the quality of life for people and our planet.

If applicable, outline how the project has the potential to make a positive impact on business performance and how the investment in professional design will result in an increase in market share, sales and/or customer loyalty.

Finally, the Jury wants to understand if the project has the potential to be an exemplar for good design in the Built Environment. Can it inspire positive change and drive greater demand for professional design and will it help contribute to a better, safer, and more prosperous future?

Supporting materials may include:

Plans and layouts of the project (in PDF format)
Date of construction completion (Project must be complete)
Exact address and location of project
Client Testimonials

Please note: A site inspection may be required at a later stage should the Jury deem this necessary.

FAQ.text:
FAQS

Our Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs) should answer any queries you may have about the Australian Good Design Awards program, processes and benefits. If you have any questions that are not answered, our team are here to help.

What are the different Australian Good Design Award Accolades?
The Australian Good Design Award accolades are among the most respected and recognised international design endorsement symbols in the industry. These registered trademarks are visible indicators of good design principles and an organisation's commitment to design excellence.

Australian Good Design Award Winner
Awarded to projects that demonstrate design excellence.

Australian Good Design Award Gold
Awarded to top five projects in each category.

Australian Good Design Award Best in Class
Awarded to best overall project in each category.

Australian Good Design Award for Sustainability
Awarded to only one project that demonstrates excellence in sustainable design.

Australian Good Design Award of the Year
Awarded to only one exemplary project from all design disciplines and categories.
How much does it cost to enter the Australian Good Design Awards?
Are there any discounts for submitting multiple entries?
What is the Winner's Package and why do I have to pay for this?
What is included in the Winner's Package?
What if I cannot afford the Registration Fee and/or Winner's Package Fee?
My entry is not designed or sold in Australia – am I still eligible to enter?
How many entries can I submit into the Australian Good Design Awards?
Can I enter a product range as a single entry?
How should I refer to my Australian Good Design Award winning project in the media and in general promotion?
There are numerous Good Design Award programs that operate in other parts of the world, how is the Australian Good Design Awards different?
When can I purchase tickets to the Australian Good Design Awards Ceremony?

关于.text:
DESIGN FOR A BRIGHTER FUTURE

Good Design Australia is an international design promotion organisation responsible for managing the annual Australian Good Design Awards and other signature design events and initiatives.

With a proud history that dates back to 1958, we remain committed to promoting the importance of design to business, industry, government and the general public and the critical role it plays in creating a better, safer and more prosperous future.

We are passionately committed to recognising, rewarding and promoting the value of design at a local, national and international level.

We manage a number of Australian State-based design award programs including the Victorian Government’s Victorian Premier’s Design Awards program.

Through the Australian Design Council, we advocate to government and industry leaders about the role and value of design to help diversify Australia’s future economy and to help embed design-led innovation into Australia’s nation-building agenda and policy settings.

The Australian Design Council was originally established as the Industrial Design Council of Australia (IDCA) in 1958 and funded by the Commonwealth Government. The Council was based on the UK Design Council model, established by Winston Churchill’s wartime government in 1944 to support Britain’s economic recovery.

Good Design Australia acknowledges the Gadigal people of the Eora Nation as the traditional custodians of the land we gather on and pay our respects to their Elders past, present, and emerging, honouring their enduring wisdom that continues to guide us in designing a more thoughtful, inclusive, and sustainable future.

As a Certified B Corp in the design industry, we’re counted among businesses that are leading a global movement for an inclusive, equitable, and regenerative economy.

WE ARE COMMITTED TO:

Promoting the value and impact of design to business and industry, government and consumers.

Helping to foster a culture of design thinking, creativity and innovation in Australia and abroad.

Showcasing and celebrating excellence in design and design-led innovation.

WE BELIEVE GOOD DESIGN IS:

A key driver of innovation, economic growth, export and productivity.

An essential link between creativity, innovation and commercial success.

The key to increasing social well-being and prosperity through empathy and insight.

Pictured: ParkingtonCC R-Series Wheels, Australian Good Design Award Winner, Engineering Design

WE ASPIRE TO:

Position design-led innovation as a national priority for Australia.

Increase the supply and demand for well-designed products, services, spaces, places and experiences among consumers and end-users of design.

Advocating for the role of design to help create a more sustainable and prosperous Australia and ultimately, a better and more balanced world.

Pictured: The Davison Collaborative, Australian Good Design Award Gold Winner, Architectural Design – Commercial and Residential Category 

IP Australia estimates the contribution to Australia’s GDP of design related industries and workers was approximately AU$67.5 billion per annum by 2018, or more than 3.5% of GDP – equivalent to the size of the construction industry.

CUSTOMER FEEDBACK

At Good Design Australia, we are dedicated to maintaining excellence in everything we do. This commitment is reflected in our ongoing efforts to review and refine our systems and processes. Our goal is to consistently provide value that meets, and ideally exceeds, the highest industry standards.

We recognise that feedback is crucial in helping us achieve this goal. Whether you have a complaint or wish to offer constructive feedback on how we can enhance our products and services, we genuinely value your input. Your insights enable us to identify areas for improvement and to implement changes that better serve our design community and stakeholders.

To submit your feedback or raise a complaint, please email us at: mail@good-design.org

We are committed to addressing your concerns promptly and will strive to respond to all communications within 48 hours.

规则.text:
TERMS AND CONDITIONS

Terms and Conditions of Entry to the Australian Good Design Awards

General

1.  By submitting an application to the Australian Good Design Awards (an Entry), the applicant acknowledges that they have read this document (these Terms and Conditions) and agrees to be bound by, and comply with, these Terms and Conditions.

2.  Good Design Australia Pty Ltd (Good Design Australia) reserves the right to amend these Terms and Conditions at any time and for any reason, and will notify applicants of any such amendments as soon as reasonably practicable. If Good Design Australia amends these Terms and Conditions after an applicant has submitted an Entry to the Australian Good Design Awards program in a way that has a material adverse impact on the applicant, the applicant may withdraw their Entry.

Entry Eligibility

3.  Good Design Australia reserves the right to refuse or disqualify any Entries that:

(a) do not comply with these Terms and Conditions;

(b) do not comply with all applicable Eligibility Criteria;

(c) in the reasonable opinion of Good Design Australia, would be likely to challenge or put into question the reputation of Good Design Australia and/or the annual Australian Good Design Awards program, which may include (without limitation) Entries that:

i. contain false, misleading or defamatory statements; and/or

ii. otherwise undermine public confidence in the integrity, independence or credibility of the Australian Good Design Awards; and/or

(d) involve any unlawful conduct or conduct that has the intention or effect of jeopardising the fair and proper conduct of the Australian Good Design Awards.

This decision is at the sole discretion of Good Design Australia, and no correspondence will be entered into.

4.  Entries that no longer meet the eligibility criteria following submission, such as a failure to meet production deadlines, must be withdrawn by the applicant and may be re-submitted the following year only. The original application fee will be credited toward this re-submission but regrettably cannot be refunded. In the event that Good Design Australia refuses an Entry on grounds unrelated to the eligibility criteria, a refund of the original application fee will be provided to the applicant. Any costs associated with entering the annual Australian Good Design Awards program are the responsibility of the applicant and under no circumstances whatsoever may a claim be brought against Good Design Australia for any such costs (whether as damages, loss, expense or otherwise).

5.  Information supplied within or in relation to the Entry must be real, correct and complete.

6.  Claims made by the applicant such as “unique”, “world-first”, “one of a kind”, “owned by” or similar must be substantiated.

7.  Good Design Australia reserves the right to request documentary or other evidence of any details related to an Entry. This may include, for example, records of any applicable patent registrations, trade mark registrations, design registrations and certifications, and intellectual property ownership.

8.  Good Design Australia reserves the right to conduct further periodic trials or request substantiation of claims, including safety and Australian and International Standards compliance.

9.  All documentation, images, audio-visual material and other subject matter submitted as part of or accompanying an Entry (the Entry Materials) must be clearly written in the English language. Incomplete, indecipherable, or illegal Entries will be deemed invalid.

Awards and Judging

10.  Winners of the Australian Good Design Award must purchase the Winner’s Package, which incurs an additional fee. The quantum of such fee will depend on the applicant’s company size. Applicants who are not able to afford this additional fee can request, in writing, financial assistance from Good Design Australia. Requests for financial assistance are considered on a case-by-case basis and Good Design Australia may require reasonable evidence to be provided.

11.  Good Design Australia may, in its sole discretion, revoke (at any time) the winning award of the Australian Good Design Awards program on the grounds outlined in clause 3(a) to (d) above. In the event that an award is revoked on any of these grounds, a partial refund of the original application fee will be provided to the applicant.

12.  The decision of the judges is final and no correspondence will be entered into. All applicants acknowledge and agree that there is an element of subjectivity in the judging criteria. Applicants irrevocably agree not to challenge the results of Australian Good Design Awards or any decision made by the judges.

Entry Materials

13.  By submitting an Entry, the applicant grants Good Design Australia a non-exclusive, royalty-free, worldwide, perpetual, irrevocable, sub-licensable licence to:

(a) refer to, reproduce, disclose, communicate, publish and otherwise use the Entry, the Entry Materials and any part of them;

(b) refer to, reproduce, disclose, communicate, publish and otherwise use the details and any images of the design(s) the subject of the Entry (the Design); and

(c) reproduce, distribute and communicate images of the Design and all other Entry Materials (including photography and AV materials), by any means and in any medium, for the purposes of promoting the applicant, the Design, Good Design Australia and/or the Australian Good Design Awards program.

14.  By submitting an Entry, applicants confirm and warrant that:

(a) they have all relevant permission(s) from parties associated with the Design – including, for example, all owners of any intellectual property subsisting in the Design, and any other parties involved with the design and development of the Design – to submit the Entry;

(b) the applicant is entitled to grant the licence in clause 13 above, and Good Design Australia and its sub-licensees are entitled to do all the things contemplated by such licence; and

(c) Good Design Australia and its sub-licensees will not, by doing anything in clause 13 above, infringe the rights (including the intellectual property rights and moral rights) of any person or be required to pay any money to any person (whether by way of damages, compensation, licence fees, royalties or otherwise).

15.  Good Design Australia will use its best endeavours to credit Entry Materials where such information has been specifically supplied. All other documentation, images, audio-visual material and other subject matter will be credited: Images courtesy of Good Design Australia.

Review and Complaints Procedure

16.  If Good Design Australia receives any complaint, or otherwise becomes aware of any issue, in relation to an applicant, an Entry, or a Design at any time, Good Design Australia may investigate the complaint or issue, including by reconsidering the Entry even if it has received an award. Complaints and issues may include, for example, allegations that the Design is counterfeit or that the owners of intellectual property in the Design had not consented to submission of the Entry.

17.  Where an Entry is subject to a complaint or investigation, the applicant must fully cooperate by providing any information and materials requested by Good Design Australia including, for example, evidence of intellectual property registrations or ownership rights. Good Design Australia may also seek information from relevant third parties and conduct its own investigations.

18.  Good Design Australia reserves the right, in its sole discretion, to refuse or disqualify any Entry (even if it has won), or revoke any Australian Good Design Award where:

(a) Good Design Australia determines that the Entry, any Entry Materials or the Design does not comply with these Terms and Conditions or is otherwise unsuitable to be awarded an Australian Good Design Award; and/or

(b) Good Design Australia is aware that the Design or any Entry Materials are the subject of a court order, final judgment or other binding legal decision finding that the Design or the Entry Materials infringe the rights (including intellectual property rights) of any person or that the applicant does not have applicable rights to the Design or Entry Materials.

19.  Good Design Australia is not a court, tribunal or other arbitrator of legal rights, and will not adjudicate or determine legal disputes between an applicant and other persons. Good Design Australia’s role in reviewing complaints is limited to assessing eligibility for participation in the Australian Good Design Awards and compliance with these Terms and Conditions. Any decision made by the Good Design Australia is for the purpose of administering the Australian Good Design Awards only and does not constitute a legal determination of ownership or other rights. Applicants acknowledge that any unresolved disputes with other persons in relation to Designs or Entry Materials must be pursued through appropriate legal channels.

Product Handling and Liability

20.  Product samples might undergo internal inspections or tests and might therefore be dismantled. Every effort will be made to rebuild product samples to their original state prior to return, however, Good Design Australia accepts no responsibility for failure to put product samples back to their original state.

21.  Except for any liability that cannot by law be excluded, Good Design Australia (including its respective officers, employees and agents) excludes all liability (including negligence), for any personal injury; or any loss or damage (including loss of opportunity); whether direct, indirect, special or consequential, arising in any way out of the Australian Good Design Awards program, including:

(a) any technical difficulties or equipment malfunction (whether or not under Good Design Australia’s control);

(b) any theft, unauthorised access or third party interference (although reasonable effort will be made to ensure the security of products and other materials submitted by the applicant during assessment and exhibition);

(c) any physical Entry Materials or product samples that are late, lost, altered, damaged or misdirected (whether or not after their receipt by Good Design Australia) due to any reason beyond the reasonable control of Good Design Australia, including during transportation, handling or storage; or

(d) any tax liability incurred by an applicant.

This limitation does not apply where the liability arises due to the gross negligence, fraud, criminal conduct or wilful misconduct of Good Design Australia or its affiliates.

22.  Due to the highly fragile nature of some physical Entry Materials, please be aware that damage or loss might occur. The packaging, transportation and insurance of any physical Entry Material is the responsibility of the applicant. Insurance to cover damage or loss should be considered if the value of the item is considerable.

23.  Physical Entry Materials are “on loan” by Good Design Australia for the period between the submission due date to the Australian Good Design Awards Presentation Ceremony. It is the responsibility of the applicant to arrange for the collection of all physical Entry Materials during the two weeks following this period should they require. Otherwise, all physical Entry Materials will be retained by Good Design Australia to do with what they deem fit, such as use in future exhibitions and promotional opportunities.

Reference to the Australian Good Design Awards

24.  Whenever a winner of an Australian Good Design Award refers to such award, in any medium and for any purpose (including, without limitation, for marketing or promotional purposes) the winner must describe the award as follows: Australian Good Design Award, Awarded by Good Design Australia

Use of Good Design Australia’s Trade Marks

The following clauses apply to use of the trade marks (the GDA Trade Marks):

25.  Good Design Australia is the registered owner of the GDA Trade Marks and reserves all rights in relation to the display and other use of such trade marks, including rights conferred by the Trade Marks Act 1995 (Cth) and under general law.

26.  A person may only use a GDA Trade Mark if Good Design Australia confirms in writing that it is granting a licence of the GDA Trade Marks to that person (the Licensee). As a pre-condition to such licence, the Licensee must:

(a) purchase the Winners Package; and

(b) agree to be bound by the terms of the licence in these Terms and Conditions, however  Good Design Australia may impose additional requirements for obtaining and maintaining the right to use the GDA Trade Marks.

27.  Such licence is non-exclusive, non-transferable, non-sublicensable and revocable.

A Licensee may only use a GDA Trade Mark:

(a) for the sole purpose of promoting the Design as the winner of an Australian Good Design Award;

(b) as supplied by Good Design Australia and without modification;

(c) alone, clearly separated from any other trade marks, logos or branding, and not as part of any combined or composite trade with any other trade marks, logos or branding; and

(d) otherwise comply with these Terms and Conditions.

28.  Good Design Australia may revoke this licence, immediately by written notice at any time, if in the opinion of Good Design Australia:

(a) the Licensee has not complied with these Terms and Conditions; and/or

(b) use of any GDA Trade Marks by or on behalf of the Licensee brings the reputation of the Australian Good Design Awards or Good Design Australia into question.

Once the licence is revoked, the Licensee must immediately cease all use of all GDA Trade Marks.

29.  If Good Design Australia grants a licence to use the GDA Trade Marks in respect of a particular Entry, and Good Design Australia disqualifies such Entry, the licence is automatically revoked and the Licensee must immediately cease all use of all GDA Trade Marks.

30.  The Licensee is required to keep Good Design Australia informed of all material changes to the Entry and the Licensee’s trading circumstances during the licence period. Good Design Australia reserves the right to make inquiries and inspections as required to confirm ongoing compliance with the licence.

31.  All usage of the GDA Trade Marks must conform with the current style manual and artwork supplied by Good Design Australia. Such artwork remains the property of Good Design Australia and must be returned immediately upon request.

32.  The Licensee is responsible for all the actions of its agents, officers, employees and contractors in respect of the GDA Trade Marks.

33.  The Licensee indemnifies Good Design Australia against any claim, loss, damage, liability, cost and expense that may be incurred or sustained by Good Design Australia caused by or arising out of:

(a) anything done by such an agent, officer, employee or contractor in relation to any GDA Trade Mark that would, if such person was the Licensee, constitute a breach of any of these Ter

参赛.text:
2025 AWARDS ENTRIES OPEN
PUBLISHED ON: 24 FEBRUARY 2025
SHARE

Design is a powerful catalyst for change – it sparks innovation, solves complex challenges, and shapes the way we live. The 2025 Australian Good Design Awards embrace this transformative potential with the theme Rewarding, Defining and Celebrating Better ______.“

This year’s theme invites designers, innovators, architects, engineers and creative thinkers to explore and articulate what “better” means in today’s world. 

By leaving the statement open-ended, the theme encourages a broad spectrum of interpretations. From better places, spaces, products, and experiences – to more specific areas like workplaces, buildings, apps, services, branding, and user interfaces – design’s potential for improvement is limitless.

The annual Awards aim to inspire entrants to showcase work that not only solves problems, but also enriches experiences, enhances sustainability, and fosters progress in all aspects of our lives.

Through this theme, the 2025 Awards reinforce their mission to not only recognise the best in design but to inspire a collective movement towards a better and more thoughtfully designed world.

We invite designers to demonstrate how their work creates measurable impact, tackles real-world challenges, and drives meaningful progress.

HOW THIS COMES TO LIFE

The 2025 Australian Good Design Awards will be a journey of discovery, celebrating the many forms of good design, from incremental improvements making our day-to-day experiences smoother, to projects driving sustainability, advancing accessibility and implementing world-first technologies that are shaping new industries. 

At the heart of this theme are three powerful words: Rewarding, Defining, and Celebrating. 

The Awards seek to Reward those who push boundaries, create impact, and set new benchmarks for excellence. They also play a crucial role in Defining what good design looks like – helping to shape industry standards and influence future design directions. Finally, the Awards Ceremony Celebrates the outstanding achievements of Award Winners, shining a light on projects that exemplify better. 

At every phase we aim to highlight how design can improve different areas of life – whether it’s Better Products, Better Places, Better Services, Better Experiences, or Better Systems. 

“This dynamic concept embodies the spirit of the Australian Good Design Awards, where design is not just about creating something new or something more, but is actually about creating something better – for people, for the planet, and for the future,” said Rachel Wye, Managing Director of Good Design Australia.

“The 2025 Awards will celebrate this vision, championing design as a force for innovation and improvement, to inspire a better tomorrow,” she added.

NEW FOR 2025: LAUNCHING THE CONCEPT DESIGN DISCIPLINE

For the 2025 Australian Good Design Awards, we’re launching an exciting new design discipline for Concept Design. 

The Concept Design discipline celebrates the power of ideas in shaping the future of design and innovation, recognising projects that push boundaries and explore new possibilities in design, technology, materials, and user experiences.

Aligned with the UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), it highlights design’s critical role in fostering a more sustainable and equitable future by encouraging innovative solutions with meaningful impact.

Open to early-stage concepts, concept cars, pre-production projects, and forward-thinking designs across all disciplines, the Concept Design discipline welcomes entries from individuals and companies from around the world. There is no age restriction to enter.

“The new Concept Design discipline celebrates the power of good ideas to shape our future. By recognising early-stage innovations that are aligned to the SDGs, we aim to inspire bold solutions that drive meaningful change. We can’t wait to see the creativity this new category will bring.” Rachel shared.

EARLY BIRD ENTRIES NOW OPEN

The Australian Good Design Awards are a global showcase of the best in design and innovation. With a proud history dating back to 1958, this prestigious program celebrates groundbreaking work across business and industry – from architecture and engineering to fashion, social impact, and everyday products and services.

Recognised by the World Design Organization (WDO), the Awards set the international benchmark for exceptional design, highlighting cutting-edge projects that push boundaries and shape the future. Each year, the Awards honour the brilliant people and ideas behind the designs transforming our world.

“We want to hear from designers who are making things ‘better’ through their own interpretation of what “better” means, and how their design contributes to a brighter future,” concluded Ms. Wye.  

Early bird entries are now open. Stay tuned to see how this dynamic theme will unfold throughout the year, culminating in the exciting 2025 Australian Good Design Awards.

Entry Portal
Timeline and Fees
Categories and Criteria
Winner’s Benefits

Key dates: 

17 March 2025 – Early Bird Deadline
02 May 2025 – Entries Deadline
May to July 2025 – Judging
17 October 2025 – Awards Ceremony, ICC Sydney
